demos.evidence_text: Monthly clusters, typically five or seven seizures over 24 h
demos.note_text:
St Mary's Hospital 

Institute of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Ahmed  
St Mary's Hospital  
Institute of Neurology, St Mary's Hospital, London W2 1NY  

Dear Dr Ahmed,  

Liam Thompson, DOB: 12-04-1991, Hospital No: NHS8435271 NHS No. 6082194753  
Flat 3B, 19 Market Street, London, W1F 8QA 

Medication: 

Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient reports good daytime tolerability; occasional transient irritability noted during high-stress shifts. Rescue: Buccal midazolam supplied; not yet administered at work.

History since last review: 

He works as a line cook in a commercial kitchen with sustained high heat, sharp implements and heavy pans. He describes Monthly clusters, typically five or seven seizures over 24 h, almost always occurring on late-shift days following consecutive long hours. Events are reported to start with a brief warning of metallic taste and a sense of heat rushing to the head, followed by impaired awareness with automatic stirring or reaching movements; colleagues have witnessed brief periods of unresponsiveness lasting 30–60 seconds. There has been no generalized convulsion reported, and recovery is usually within minutes, though he feels fatigued and slow for several hours afterwards. He notes dehydration and missed meals during service as common antecedents; sleep restriction after double shifts is frequent. No clear intercurrent illness or alcohol trigger. 

Workplace and safety: 

Episodes have occurred at the sauté and grill stations with open flames. He and his manager have implemented a buddy system during late service, immediate step-back from the line at the earliest aura, and relocation to a cool rest area. Knives and hot pans are handed off when a warning is sensed. He wears heat-resistant gloves routinely and avoids high-level ladder access. No injuries to date. He has not required emergency attendance; colleagues time events and ensure supervised rest. 

Plan: 

- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. If the monthly clusters persist at the current pattern, he will keep a precise diary of shift times, sleep duration, hydration, and aura occurrence to guide potential dose optimisation.  
- Maintain ready access to buccal midazolam per existing workplace protocol; training for two additional senior colleagues has been scheduled by his employer.  
- Encourage regular hydration and scheduled carbohydrate intake before peak service; he has arranged short breaks at 18:00 and 20:00 on late shifts.  
- We will obtain updated bloods (U&Es, LFTs) and review irritability symptoms; if troublesome, we can consider mitigation strategies.  
- Provide a brief written workplace risk summary for his manager to reinforce existing precautions. 

Follow-up: 

We will see him again in four months, sooner if cluster frequency increases beyond the described monthly pattern or if any convulsive events occur. 

Yours sincerely
demos.seizure_frequency_number: 1 cluster per month, 5 to 7 per cluster

demos.evidence_text: He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015
demos.note_text:
Department of Neurology

Clinic Date: 18 July 2016

Dr A. Patel
St Cuthbert’s Neurology Clinic
Level 3, Outpatients, St Cuthbert’s Hospital, York YO1 7AB

Dear Dr A. Patel,

Michael Turner, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9437710256
Flat 12, 5 Mill Lane, York, YO10 3BG

Diagnoses:

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology not established. Prior EEG in community reportedly non-diagnostic.

Current Medication:

Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily

Plan:

Continue current antiepileptic medication. Reinforce sleep hygiene and trigger management. Safety counselling and personalised plan held on record.

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports that he has been Seizure-free since 13-Nov-2015. Prior to that date he described occasional cluster patterns associated with sleep loss, and he has since focused on regular sleep routines and avoiding sleep deprivation. He also confirms that a personalised self-management and safety plan is documented elsewhere in his notes and remains up to date.

On examination today there are no focal neurological deficits. He is tolerating Lamotrigine 75 mg twice daily without adverse effects. Given the current stability and absence of events since November 2015, I have advised continuing the present regimen and maintaining vigilance around recognised triggers, particularly sleep loss. He has a documented plan for what to do should clusters recur, and he understands the escalation steps.

I have not arranged a routine follow-up; however, I am happy to review him sooner if there are any breakthrough events or medication concerns. Otherwise, we can consider review in due course to reassess treatment duration in the context of ongoing stability.

With best wishes,

demos.evidence_text: he remains clinically stable and Free of seizures for 1 year
demos.note_text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Patel
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: KCH728311 NHS No. 9468201573  
12 Rowan Close, Bromley, BR2 8LP

Thank you for asking me to review your patient. He works rotating shifts, including nights, and reports long-standing sleep disturbance with variable bedtimes and fragmented rest. Despite the circadian rhythm disruption, he has been Free of seizures for 1 year by his account and corroborated by his partner and workplace records (no incidents reported on occupational health logs). There have been no witnessed collapses, no nocturnal events, and no injuries or tongue biting in that period. He denies any recent auras, automatisms, or post-event confusion. He carries a personal seizure action plan but has not needed to use it over the last year. ï¿½

He does describe episodic fatigue on switching from nights to early shifts, transient difficulty with concentration, and occasional headaches at the end of a run of nights. These appear temporally linked to sleep restriction rather than to paroxysmal events. There is no new focal neurological symptomatology. He remains on a stable dose of his regular anti-seizure medication and is fully adherent; there have been no recent dose changes, drug interactions, or missed doses. Occupationally he has continued in his role without incident, and managerial reports confirm punctual attendance and no safety alerts. Ã©

On examination today he was alert and cooperative. Speech, language, and cognition were grossly intact on bedside testing. Cranial nerves were normal. Motor and sensory examinations were unremarkable. Coordination and gait were normal. Cardiovascular observations were stable. There were no stigmata of medication toxicity. 

Investigations: Routine bloods earlier this month (FBC, U&E, LFTs, bone profile, vitamin D) were within reference ranges. ECG today shows normal sinus rhythm with no conduction delay. Prior MRI brain and EEG (from last year) did not yield a unifying diagnosis. I note that clinical classification remains under review; at present I cannot assign a definitive seizure syndrome. â€

Assessment: In the context of ongoing rotating shift work with disrupted circadian rhythm, he remains clinically stable and Free of seizures for 1 year. There is no current evidence of breakthrough events. The principal risks at present relate to sleep debt and fatigue rather than active paroxysmal activity. 

Plan:
- Continue current anti-seizure medication at the same dose; no changes today. 
- Sleep and shift hygiene: patient has implemented scheduled dark-phase sleep after night shifts, gradual phase shifts where possible, and limited caffeine after midday; he will continue these measures. 
- Occupational health: he will share this letter with his employer. Current duties can continue as is; no specific restrictions requested from me at this time. 
- Safety: standard precautions maintained; he carries an employer-notified care plan though unused for 12 months. 
- Follow-up: review in 6–9 months, or sooner if there is any recurrence, injury, loss of awareness, or concerning nocturnal behaviour reported. 

Please let me know if there is additional information from primary care or occupational health that would help longitudinal monitoring. 

Yours sincerely

King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 15 July 2018 

Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Priya Nair
Consultant Neurologist,  

Samuel Carter, DOB: 14-03-1982, Hospital No: KCH772194 NHS No. 9462013857  
12 Brookside Close, London, SE15 3PL

Thank you for asking me to review your patient in the neurology clinic. He attended today with his partner and brought his seizure diary and VNS card. He reports that his overall stability has been good since his last review and he is engaging well with routine follow-up. Seizure-free period now 14 months. He attributes this improvement to consistent medication adherence and the implanted vagus nerve stimulator (VNS). He has an implanted VNS; there were no parameter changes at this review. He uses the magnet rarely and only for reassurance; there have been no magnet-triggered events in the past year. He denies any nocturnal events, tongue biting, injuries, or emergency attendances. There has been no loss of awareness reported by him or observed by family over this period. 

He describes occasional brief spells of light-headedness when overtired, without behavioural change, automatisms, or post-event confusion; these are infrequent and self-limiting, and he has not associated them with triggers aside from sleep deprivation. He has not driven and remains compliant with DVLA guidance. Sleep and mood are reported as stable, with mild anticipatory anxiety around anniversaries of previous episodes, managed through simple relaxation strategies. There is no current concern for cognitive decline, and he has returned to part-time work without difficulties. 

Examination today was unremarkable. Cardiovascular and neurological examinations were within normal limits. VNS generator site is well healed without tenderness or skin breakdown. Device interrogation confirmed satisfactory battery status and lead impedance within expected range. No adjustments were made to stimulation parameters. 

Plan: 
- Continue current antiseizure medication at existing doses (unchanged today) and maintain VNS parameters.  
- Reinforce sleep hygiene and ongoing seizure diary entries.  
- Routine follow-up in 6 months, or sooner if any events recur or adverse effects arise.  
- Patient is aware to contact the epilepsy specialist nurse should there be any change in pattern or concerns about the device. 

Thank you for your continued care. Please let me know if there is anything further you would like me to address. 

Yours sincerely

fields.description: Seizure frequency in Gan label format. One of: '{count} per {unit}' e.g. '2 per week', '1 per 3 month'; '{count} cluster per {unit}, {n} per cluster'; 'unknown'; 'no seizure frequency reference'; or null to abstain. Unit must be singular: day/week/month/year.
Shortest exact contiguous quote from the note supporting the label. Do not include prompt instructions or wrapper text. Null if abstaining.

signature.instructions:
Extract the Gan seizure-frequency label and supporting evidence from a clinical note.

/no_think
Do not use hidden reasoning. Emit only the requested output fields.

The label must match the Gan annotation vocabulary exactly. Abstain (null) only when
the note contains no usable seizure-frequency information.

Synthesis-backed policy:
- Output only canonical Gan labels: N per unit, N to M per unit, N per M unit,
  N cluster per unit, M per cluster, seizure free for N unit, unknown, or
  no seizure frequency reference.
- Use singular units: day, week, month, year.
- Convert daily/nightly to 1 per day, every N unit to 1 per N unit, and
  numeric "or" ranges to "to" ranges.
- Choose the highest current quantified seizure-type frequency.
- Use the full cluster format; do not drop either the cluster period or
  the per-cluster count.
- Use seizure free for N unit only when seizure freedom is at least 6 months.
  If seizure freedom is shorter than 6 months, compute the rate from the
  total events over the described period.
- For year-to-date counts, use months elapsed since January as the denominator.
- Treat "over the last quarter" as an observation window when the note gives
  a per-month rate; do not automatically convert it to per 3 month.
- evidence_text must be an exact contiguous quote from the note.
